course_info.title: Calculus I
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Evaluate limits using algebraic techniques and L'Hopital's rule
Determine continuity of functions at a point and on an interval
Understand the epsilon-delta definition of limits

lecture_notes.introduction: Calculus is the mathematics of change. Limits formalize the notion of approaching a value, continuity ensures functions behave predictably, and derivatives measure instantaneous rates of change. These concepts underpin machine learning optimization, graphics, physics simulation, and signal processing.
key_concepts: Intuitive and formal (epsilon-delta) definition of limits
Limit laws: sum, product, quotient, squeeze theorem
One-sided limits and limits at infinity
Continuity at a point: limit exists, function defined, they are equal
Intermediate Value Theorem: continuous functions hit every value between endpoints
lecture_notes.demonstration: Visualize limits graphically with a function that has a removable discontinuity, a jump discontinuity, and an asymptote, computing each limit algebraically and confirming with the graph

review.value: For every epsilon > 0, there exists a delta > 0 such that if 0 < |x - a| < delta then |f(x) - L| < epsilon; this formalizes 'f(x) can be made arbitrarily close to L by making x sufficiently close to a'

Continuous functions are important in optimization (gradient descent assumes continuity), interpolation (smooth curves between data points), and numerical methods (root-finding algorithms require continuity)

learning_objectives: Compute derivatives using the limit definition
Apply differentiation rules: power, product, quotient, chain
Interpret the derivative as rate of change and slope of tangent line

lecture_notes.introduction: The derivative measures instantaneous rate of change. It tells you how fast a function is changing at any point, which is exactly what gradient descent needs to know: in which direction and how steeply should we adjust parameters to minimize a loss function?
key_concepts: Definition: f'(x) = lim h->0 [f(x+h) - f(x)] / h
Power rule: d/dx[x^n] = n*x^(n-1)
Product rule: (fg)' = f'g + fg'
Quotient rule: (f/g)' = (f'g - fg') / g^2
Chain rule: d/dx[f(g(x))] = f'(g(x)) * g'(x)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Derive the power rule from the limit definition, then use the chain rule to differentiate a neural network activation function (sigmoid), showing how backpropagation is just the chain rule applied repeatedly
setup_requirements: Python with SymPy for symbolic differentiation

review.value: Backpropagation in neural networks is the chain rule applied to composite functions: the gradient of the loss with respect to each weight is computed by multiplying partial derivatives along the computation graph from output to input
At a point x=a, f(x) is approximately f(a) + f'(a)(x-a); this linear approximation is the basis of Newton's method for root finding and first-order optimization methods

class_schedule.topic: Applications of Derivatives: Optimization and Curve Sketching
learning_objectives: Find critical points and classify them as maxima, minima, or saddle points
Apply the first and second derivative tests
Solve optimization problems from real-world scenarios

lecture_notes.introduction: Optimization is finding the best solution: the maximum profit, the minimum cost, the shortest path, the lowest loss. Calculus provides the tools to find these optima by setting the derivative to zero and classifying the critical points.
key_concepts: Critical points: where f'(x) = 0 or f'(x) is undefined
First derivative test: sign changes determine max vs min
Second derivative test: f''(c) > 0 means minimum, f''(c) < 0 means maximum
Absolute extrema on closed intervals: check critical points and endpoints
Applied optimization: formulate, differentiate, solve, verify
lecture_notes.demonstration: Optimize the dimensions of a shipping box with a fixed surface area to maximize volume, then connect to how gradient descent finds the minimum of a loss function

review.value: Gradient descent iteratively moves in the direction of steepest decrease (negative gradient) to find a minimum; the learning rate controls step size, and the second derivative (Hessian) reveals the curvature of the loss surface

Setting f'(x) = 0 finds local optima; for convex functions (f''(x) > 0 everywhere), every local minimum is the global minimum. Non-convex functions (like neural network loss surfaces) have multiple local minima

class_schedule.topic: Integration and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
learning_objectives: Compute definite and indefinite integrals using basic rules
Apply the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus to evaluate definite integrals
Interpret the definite integral as accumulated change and area under the curve

lecture_notes.introduction: Integration is the inverse of differentiation. While derivatives measure instantaneous change, integrals accumulate change over an interval. Integration computes areas, volumes, probabilities, and expected values — all essential for statistics and machine learning.
key_concepts: Antiderivatives: F'(x) = f(x), the reverse of differentiation
Basic integration rules: power rule, sum rule, constant multiple
Definite integral: accumulating f(x) from a to b
Fundamental Theorem: integral from a to b of f(x)dx = F(b) - F(a)
Substitution method: the chain rule in reverse
lecture_notes.demonstration: Compute the area under a probability density function to find the probability of an event, then use substitution to integrate a composition

review.value: The Fundamental Theorem connects derivatives and integrals: differentiation and integration are inverse operations. If you differentiate an integral, you get back the original function; if you integrate a derivative, you get the net change

The probability that a continuous random variable falls in an interval [a,b] equals the integral of its density function from a to b; the total integral over all values must equal 1. Expected value E[X] = integral of x*f(x)dx

class_schedule.topic: Applications of Integration and Numerical Methods
learning_objectives: Apply integration to compute areas between curves and volumes of revolution
Approximate integrals using numerical methods: trapezoidal rule, Simpson's rule
Understand when numerical integration is necessary and its error bounds

lecture_notes.introduction: Many integrals have no closed-form solution. Numerical integration approximates these integrals to any desired accuracy. This is how computers evaluate integrals in practice, and understanding the methods and their error bounds is essential for computational mathematics.
key_concepts: Area between curves: integral of [f(x) - g(x)] dx
Volume of revolution: disk and shell methods
Trapezoidal rule: approximate area using trapezoids, error O(h^2)
Simpson's rule: approximate using parabolas, error O(h^4)
Error bounds: how many subdivisions for a given accuracy
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implement the trapezoidal rule and Simpson's rule in Python, compare their accuracy on a function with a known integral, and plot the error vs number of subdivisions
setup_requirements: Function with known analytical integral for comparison

review.value: Simpson's rule uses parabolic approximation and achieves O(h^4) error, meaning doubling the number of intervals reduces error by a factor of 16; it is exact for polynomials up to degree 3

Many machine learning computations (marginalizing over latent variables, computing expected values, normalizing probability distributions) require integrals that have no closed form; Monte Carlo integration and quadrature methods handle these computationally

questions.question: Compute the derivative of f(x) = (3x^2 + 1)^4 * sin(x) using the product and chain rules. Show all steps.
Find the limit: lim(x->0) [sin(3x) / (2x)]. Show your reasoning.
questions.answer:
lim(x->0) sin(3x)/(2x) = lim(x->0) [sin(3x)/(3x)] * (3x)/(2x) = lim(x->0) [sin(3x)/(3x)] * (3/2) = 1 * (3/2) = 3/2.

Using the fundamental limit lim(u->0) sin(u)/u = 1 with u = 3x.

rubric.key: Correct manipulation to use sin(u)/u limit

questions.question: A farmer has 200 meters of fencing to enclose a rectangular field next to a river (no fence needed along the river). What dimensions maximize the enclosed area? Prove it is a maximum using the second derivative test.
questions.answer: Let x = width (perpendicular to river), y = length (parallel to river). Constraint: 2x + y = 200, so y = 200 - 2x. Area A = xy = x(200-2x) = 200x - 2x^2. A'(x) = 200 - 4x = 0 gives x = 50. A''(x) = -4 < 0, confirming this is a maximum. y = 200 - 2(50) = 100. Maximum area = 50 * 100 = 5000 square meters.

questions.question: Evaluate the definite integral: integral from 0 to 2 of x * e^(x^2) dx using substitution.
questions.answer:
Let u = x^2, then du = 2x dx, so x dx = du/2.
When x = 0: u = 0. When x = 2: u = 4.

Integral = integral from 0 to 4 of (1/2) * e^u du = (1/2)[e^u] from 0 to 4 = (1/2)(e^4 - e^0) = (e^4 - 1)/2 approximately equal to 26.80.

major_assignments.title: Calculus Applications in Computer Science
major_assignments.description: Apply calculus concepts to three CS-relevant problems: (1) implement and analyze gradient descent for a simple optimization problem, (2) use integration to compute probabilities from a continuous distribution, and (3) implement and compare numerical integration methods with error analysis.
objectives: Implement gradient descent using derivatives for optimization
Implement numerical integration methods and analyze their errors
Connect calculus theory to computational applications
deliverables: Python implementations of all three problems
Mathematical derivations showing all calculus work
Analysis report (3-4 pages) connecting calculus to CS applications
Plots showing convergence, distributions, and numerical errors

criteria: Derivative computation is correct
Gradient descent converges to the correct minimum
Learning rate analysis shows effect on convergence
Plots show loss curve and parameter trajectory
Integral setup is correct for the given distribution
Analytical solution computed correctly
Numerical verification matches analytical result
Visualization of the density function and probability area
Trapezoidal and Simpson rules implemented correctly
Error analysis compares methods at different resolutions
Convergence rates match theoretical predictions
Clear presentation of results
Mathematical derivations are complete and correct
Code is documented and reproducible
Report connects theory to CS applications
Professional formatting
Correct gradient descent implementation with convergence analysis
Accurate probability computation using integration
Numerical methods with proper error analysis
Clear documentation connecting calculus to CS

homework_assignments.description: Weekly problem sets with computations and proofs

major_assignments.description: Application project connecting calculus to CS

midterm_examination.description: Written exam covering limits, derivatives, and optimization

final_examination.description: Comprehensive exam covering all calculus topics

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is expected for all lectures. More than 3 unexcused absences may reduce participation grade.
course_policies.late_submissions: Late homework loses 10% per day. No submissions accepted more than 5 days late.
course_policies.collaboration: Discussion of approaches is encouraged. All written solutions must be your own work.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All submitted work must be your own. Copying from solution manuals or other students is plagiarism.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students need a graphing calculator (physical or Desmos) and Python with NumPy and matplotlib for the application project.
